    CODEC Data CODEC Metadata Working with a Tabular Data Resource in R

CODEC Data

Source: vignettes/codec-data.Rmd
codec-data.Rmd

CODEC Data Standards

Language: The keywords “MUST”, “MUST NOT”, “SHOULD”, “SHOULD NOT”, and “MAY” in this document are to be interpreted as described in RFC 2119.

Files

A CODEC tabular-data-resource (i.e., tdr) consists of a directory that MUST contain exactly one data (.csv) file and exactly one metadata file (tabular-data-resource.yaml).

The name of the directory and the name of the CSV file containing the data MUST be identical to the name property.

For example,

mydata
├── mydata.csv
└── tabular-data-resource.yaml

Both files MUST be encoded using UTF-8 with newlines encoded as either \n or \r\n.

Metadata YAML file

The metadata file MUST be a YAML file named tabular-data-resource.yaml and it MUST adhere to the CODEC Metadata Standards.

Data CSV file

The data file must follow the RFC 4180 standard for CSV files. In addition:

  • the filename must end with .csv
  • the first row MUST be a header row, containing the unique name of each field

Missing Values

If a value is missing, it MUST be represented by either NA or an empty string (``) in the CSV data file.

Census Tract Geography

A census tract column MUST be specified in metadata and named census_tract_id_{year}, where {year} is replaced with the decennial vintage of the census tract geographies used to create the dataset (e.g., census_tract_id_2010 or census_tract_id_2020).

The census tract identifier column MUST contain 11-digit GEOID identifiers for all census tracts in Hamilton County (GEOID: 39061). A list of required census tract identifiers for 2000, 2010, and 2020 are available in the {{cincy}} R package (e.g., cincy::tract_tigris_2010).

A CODEC tdr that was not created at a census tract level SHOULD link to a URL that contains code and a descriptive README file about how the data was harmonized (e.g., areal interpolation) with census tract geographies.

Date / Date Range?

  • data must correspond to a year or month
  • or require start_date and end_date and assume value represents temporal average of this time period

On this page

Developed by Cole Brokamp, Erika Manning.
